segments.s519-g97: بدو is the dative/enclitic pronoun 'to him' placed before the verb; داد is the simple past transitive 'gave'. پس is an adverb 'then' qualifying the action. دختر خویش را is the definite direct object: دختر 'daughter' + the possessive enclitic ش (written here split as خوی + ش) meaning 'his/own', and را is the Persian definite object marker. Word order here places the indirect object (بدو) before the verb and the marked direct object after it.
segments.s519-g98: پسندیده is an adjective/participial modifier 'chosen, approved, pleasing' that refers back to 'دختر' in the previous segment; the verb داد is omitted here by ellipsis and understood to repeat. و 'and' links the two objects, so افسر خویش را is a second definite object ('afsar' in classical poetic usage = helmet/crown) with the possessive enclitic ش and object marker را, completing what was (again) given.
segments.s519-g81: A compact noun phrase: نبیره (scion/offspring) is head of the phrase and is followed by the titles/epithets that identify him. جهاندار شاه اردشیر is a string of modifiers appended to نبیره: جهاندار is an epithet ('world‑holder' / sovereign) qualifying شاه (king), and اردشیر is the proper name; the order is head (نبیره) then post‑modifying titles and name.
segments.s519-g82: که introduces a relative clause 'whom/that'. بهمنش = بهمن + -ش (3rd‑person clitic) means 'him as Bahman' — i.e. 'whom you call Bahman.' خواندی here is a 2nd‑person past (or historic) of خواندن with the sense 'call/name' (in epic contexts it can have a habitual/presentive force when combined with همی). همی is the archaic continuous/iterative particle emphasizing ongoing/habitual action. یادگیر is an imperative (= 'take to memory, remember/learn') addressed to the listener; grammatically it closes the sentence as the verb of admonition.
segments.s519-g83: Another nominal phrase: سرافراز is a prefixed epithet ('honored/lofty') modifying the following noun cluster. پور یل (son, hero) names the relationship and quality before the proper name: پور (=son/descendant) links to the personal name that follows, and یل apposes the name to indicate heroism; اسفندیار is the proper name in apposition.
segments.s519-g84: ز introduces origin/lineage ('from' or 'of') so ز گشتاسپ یل reads 'a hero from/of Gushtasp.' در جهان یادگار is a locative predicative phrase: در جهان ('in the world') and یادگار ('a memorial/remembrance'), describing him as a lasting memorial in the world. Word order is poetic: origin + ancestor + epithet, then locative + predicate noun.
segments.s519-g85: چو introduces a temporal clause ('when'). بشنید is 3rd‑person past 'he heard'; the proper name بابک is the subject placed after the verb (inversion common in poetry). فرو ریخت آب is a verb phrase with the separable adverbial فرو ('down') + past verb ریخت ('poured'), followed by the noun آب (water) as the thing that poured — literally 'water poured down.'
segments.s519-g86: ازان = 'from that/therefrom' referring back to what flowed; چشم روشن is a noun phrase 'bright eye' (چشم head, روشن adjective). که introduces a relative clause 'which/that'; او is the clause subject 'he', دید is past 'saw' and خواب follows as the object meaning 'dream' — 'which he had seen in a dream.' Word order places the object (خواب) after the verb, idiomatic in epic Persian.
segments.s519-g87: بیاورد is 3rd‑person past 'he brought' (or 'he produced'). پس functions as a discourse adverb 'then/after that.' جامه پهلوی is a noun phrase: جامه (garment/robe) modified by پهلوی; here پهلوی is adjectival/ethnic — 'Pahlavi' or 'of Pahlavi style' (i.e. Middle‑Persian dress).
segments.s519-g88: یکی باره is an idiom meaning 'all at once / immediately' (یکی = one, باره = occasion/instance, together = 'in one instance'). با آلت خسروی is a prepositional phrase 'with the royal instruments/implements' where آلت is 'instrument/implement' and خسروی is an adjective 'royal/kingly'; the whole phrase indicates he brought the robe together with the royal regalia.
segments.s519-g49: پراندیشه شد is a compound: پراندیشه (an adjective/nominative phrase meaning 'pensive/filled with thought') + شد (past-tense auxiliary 'became'). زان سخن is a short prepositional phrase 'because of/at that speech' (زان = 'from that; because of that'; سخن = 'speech'). The whole segment means 'became pensive because of that speech.'
segments.s519-g50: نهاده is a past participle meaning 'having placed/put' (here 'having put/placed'). برو is used poetically as a directional/verb element 'toward' or 'turned toward' (literally 'go/turn toward'), governing گوش 'ear' (literal body-part used to mean 'listening/attention'). پاسخ‌سرای names the one who answers/responds. Lit. 'having set (his) ear toward the responder.'
segments.s519-g51: سرانجام گفت is an adverb + verb phrase where سرانجام ('finally') modifies گفت ('said/spoke'); ای سرافراز شاه is a vocative: ای ('O') + سرافراز ('honored/exalted') + شاه ('king'). The phrase opens the speech with a formal address.
segments.s519-g52: This line uses inverted, somewhat poetic word order. The neutral order would be 'باید به تأویل این نگاه کرد' — 'one must regard this as an interpretation.' به تأویل ('as/regarding in the interpretation') + این ('this') + کرد (the infinitive/verb 'to do/look' here functioning with نگاه) + باید ('it is necessary/must') + نگاه ('view/regard'). نگاه is a noun used with کردن to mean 'to regard/interpret.'
segments.s519-g53: کسی را که بینند is a relative construction: کسی را ('someone' with object/topic marker را) + که ('who/that') + بینند (relative verb form 'they see' or 'is seen' — present subjunctive/optative used here as a relative). زین سان به خواب is an adverbial phrase 'in such a manner/in such a dream' (زین سان = 'in this way'; به خواب = 'in a dream'). The clause means 'someone whom they see in a dream in this way.'
segments.s519-g54: به شاهی برآرد is a verb phrase meaning 'will bring to kingship / will raise to kingship' (برآرد here = 'attain/bring forth, effect'). سر از آفتاب is a pictorial phrase 'head from the sun' — i.e. 'his head reaching the sun' or 'his head above the sun' — with سر ('head') + از ('from/of') + آفتاب ('sun'). Word order is poetic: verb before its phrase and the figurative complement after.
segments.s519-g55: ور is the conditional 'if.' ایدونک is an archaic/poetic particle here (a time/contingency particle; glossed as uncertain) modifying the condition — roughly 'if now/by chance/if it should be so.' این خواب 'this dream' is the subject. ز + و together represent the older form 'از او' ('from him'); بگذرد is a verb 'passes/departs' (subjunctive/optative sense), so the clause reads 'if this dream departs from him.'
segments.s519-g56: پسر باشدش is a short clause meaning 'a son will be his' or 'he will have a son' (باشد + ـش enclitic = 'it will be to him'). کز ('that/which') introduces a relative clause. جهان بر خورد is a verbal phrase where بر (literal 'on/upon') + خورد ('befall/happen' here) together mean 'will fall upon / will come upon the world' — idiomatically 'will seize/affect the world' or 'will prevail over the world.' Thus: 'he will have a son who will come upon (or seize) the world.'
segments.s519-g73: This clause begins with the conjunction/relative که ("that"). با من ("with me") is an oblique prepositional phrase marking the person affected; من is object-like here. نسازی is negative (ن‑ + سازی) in a 1st/2nd/3rd‑person-present/subjunctive form used here as a promise/assurance meaning “you will not do” or “do not do.” بدی (literally “evil”) functions as the direct object meaning “harm.” در جهان is a locative phrase “in the world.”
segments.s519-g74: This is a paired negative formula using نه ... و نه ("neither ... nor"). بر آشکار is an adverbial phrase meaning “in the open/openly” (بر used in a poetic locative sense), and اندر نهان means “in the hidden” = “secretly.” The two parts are parallel adverbial complements modifying نسازی/بدی in the previous clause.
segments.s519-g75: چو بشنید is a temporal subordinate clause (چو = “when”) with the past verb بشنید (“he heard”). In poetic word order the subject بابک follows the verb. زبان برگشاد is a light-verb expression (زبان برگشودن) meaning “opened his tongue,” i.e. began to speak.
segments.s519-g76: ز یزدان نیکی دهش is a genitive chain “the gift of goodness from God” (ز = poetic “from/of”). کرد یاد is a compound where کرد (past of کردن) plus یاد yields the verbal sense “he remembered” or “he caused remembrance” — here simply “he remembered.” The whole clause reports his remembering of God’s gracious gift.
segments.s519-g77: که introduces indirect speech/content (“that …”). بر تو places the person affected before the verb (prepositional/benefactive use of بر = “against/to”), and نسازم is first‑person singular with negative prefix ن‑ meaning “I will not do” or “I will not cause.” به چیزی گزند is the object phrase meaning “any harm” (به چیزی = “by/with anything” used idiomatically as “by anything”).
segments.s519-g78: بدارمت is the verb بدارم + the enclitic object ‑ت (“you”) and functions here as a promise “I will keep/hold you.” The following adjectives شادان‌دل و ارجمند describe state: “cheerful/joyful‑hearted and honoured/esteemed.” Word order places the predicate before these adjectival complements for emphasis.
segments.s519-g79: به بابک places the indirect object/adressee up front (“to Babak”). چنین گفت is the fixed phrase “said thus.” زان پس is a temporal phrase “thereafter/after that,” and جوان (here ‘the young man’) is the speaker; poetic word order permits these elements to appear after گفت for emphasis.
segments.s519-g80: که introduces the content of what he said. من پور ساسانم is a nominal identification: من (I) + پور (son/descendant) + ساسانم (with the suffix ‑م, “I am of Sasan”) = “I am a son/descendant of Sasan.” ای پهلوان is a vocative address (“O champion”), with ای marking direct address.
segments.s519-g41: Literally ‘the head of Babak became awake’ — Persian often uses سر (‘head’) as the subject for ‘wake up’. از خواب + بیدار شد is a past action: بیدار شد is a compound of the adjective بیدار and the past tense شد (here ‘became’ → ‘woke’). Word order places the locative/phrase of origin (از خواب) before the predicate.
segments.s519-g42: روان و دلش is a coordinated subject (‘his spirit and his heart’), with the possessive suffix ش attached to دل. پر ز تیمار شد is an adjectival phrase ‘became full of care’: پر (full) + ز (from/of, poetic) + تیمار (care) + شد (became). The whole phrase describes an internal state resulting from waking.
segments.s519-g43: هرانکس که introduces a generic relative clause ‘whoever/anyone who…’. در خواب دانا بدند places the state ‘wise in sleep’ inside that clause; دانا is predicative and بدند is an archaic past/optative plural form (here rendering a general, proverbial statement: ‘they would be/are’).
segments.s519-g44: به هر دانشی is a prepositional phrase ‘in/with respect to every knowledge’; بر توانا بدند uses بر to mark the domain (‘over/in’), توانا is predicate ‘able/powerful’, and بدند repeats the archaic plural copula meaning ‘they would be’—so the clause says such people would be capable in every art/knowledge.
segments.s519-g45: به ایوان بابک is a locative prepositional phrase (‘to/at Babak’s iwan/portico’) placed before the verb. شدند انجمن uses شدند (past plural of شدن) with انجمن as the noun: literally ‘an assembly came to be/assembled’ — classical Persian often allows this inversion with the verb following the noun and its location phrase preceding it.
segments.s519-g46: This is a noun phrase listing the attendees: بزرگان (the great/elders) modified by the adjective/epithet فرزانه (wise/sage-like), and رای زن is a compound meaning ‘those who give counsel’ (an agentive construction). The conjunction و links the attributes/roles; note that زن here is not ‘woman’ but part of an older agentive expression (‘one who …’).
segments.s519-g47: چو here is the temporal conjunction ‘when’. سخن برگشاد is a verbal/adjectival phrase ‘opened/uttered speech’ (برگشاد = ‘unveiled/revealed’). از نهفت is a prepositional phrase ‘from that which was hidden’ (نهفت used as a noun/adjective ‘hidden’), so the clause means ‘when Babak revealed what was hidden in his speech.’
segments.s519-g48: همه خواب یکسر is the object: همه خواب ‘the whole dream’ and یکسر (here) ‘at once/entirely’. بدیشان is the dative cliticized form ‘to them’ and بگفت is the classical past tense ‘he told/said’. The finite verb bگفت places the action squarely in the past.
segments.s519-g33: به ... شب‌اندر is a temporal prepositional phrase: به (preposition) + دیگر (another) + شب‌اندر (at night) = "on another night." چو introduces a temporal clause "when," and بخفت is the past tense verb (classical/poetic past of خوابیدن) placed at the end as in Persian: "when Babak slept."
segments.s519-g34: همی بود is a habitual/persistent past construction: همی (adverb = 'ever/continually') + بود (past of بودن) = 'was always.' با مغزش اندیشه جفت is a poetic word order: با (with) + مغزش (his brain) + اندیشه (thought) + جفت (pair). Here جفت functions as a predicative noun/adjective meaning 'paired/paired with,' so the phrase says his brain and thought were continuously paired (i.e. his mind was full of thought).
segments.s519-g35: چنان (thus/so) modifies the verb دید (saw). در خواب ('in a dream') is an adverbial phrase indicating where/when the vision occurred. کاتش‌پرست is the direct object (the figure seen): 'a fire‑worshipper.' The order (adverb + verb + prep. phrase + object) is poetic but grammatical.
segments.s519-g36: سه آتش is the object phrase 'three fires.' ببردی is an archaic/poetic past form of بردن meaning 'bore/carried' (here rendered 'carried' or 'was bearing'). فروزان is an adjective 'radiant/blazing' describing the fires. به دست is a locative phrase 'in (his) hand' (به + دست). Word order places the verb between the object and its modifiers as is common in poetry.
segments.s519-g37: چو is the comparative/simile marker 'like/as.' آذر گشسپ together names a famed sacred fire (Azar‑Goshasp) and is treated as a unit; گشسپ (Goshasp) is a proper name modifying/identifying that fire. The repeated چو introduces additional comparisons (چو خراد و مهر = 'like Khraad and Mehr'). The sequence lists models for the fires' appearance.
segments.s519-g38: فروزان is an adjectival/participial form 'radiant' describing the fires. به کردار is a fixed phrase meaning 'in the manner/appearance of' (به + کردار). گردان سپهر is a noun phrase 'the revolving sky/heavens' (گردان = revolving, سپهر = sky); the whole phrase says they were radiant 'in the form/mode of the turning heavens.'
segments.s519-g39: همه is the object 'all/the whole lot.' پیش ساسان is a prepositional phrase 'before/in the presence of Sasan' (پیش = before/in front of). فروزان is an attributive adjective 'radiant' qualifying the things presented. بدی here occurs as a verb form meaning 'gave/presented,' but that verbal sense is archaic/poetic (see note on senses). The clause thus reads 'he presented them all, glowing, before Sasan.'
segments.s519-g40: به هر آتشی is a dative phrase 'to each fire' (به + هر + آتشی). عود سوزان is a noun phrase 'burning agarwood/oud' (عود = agarwood, سوزان = burning/fiery) indicating what was offered. The final بدی is the same archaic/poetic verb 'gave/put' understood with an implied subject; word order (dative + object + verb) is typical of the elevated diction.
segments.s519-g1: چو is a temporal conjunction meaning “when.” دارا here is a proper name (Dārā); Persian proper names can appear without a special particle. به رزم اندرون places the action in a location/scene: به + noun can indicate ‘into/toward’ and رزم ‘battle,’ while اندرون specifies ‘within/inner (quarters)’—word order is preposition + place + modifier. کشته شد is a passive past construction: کشته (past participle “killed”) + شد (auxiliary “became/was”), yielding “was killed.”
segments.s519-g2: همه دوده را is fronted for emphasis; همه “all/every” modifies دوده “soot/smoke.” را is the definite/object/topic marker attached to دوده. روز برگشته شد is a personified/intransitive expression: روز “day” + برگشته (perfect/participle ‘returned/turned back’) + شد (auxiliary), literally “the day was turned back/returned.” The whole segment pictures daylight turning back over/against the soot (poetic inversion).
segments.s519-g3: This clause uses an inverted/breviated word order typical of Persian epic style: پسر “son” is the head noun. بد here is archaic/poetic and intensifying/qualifying (traditionally can hint at ill/tragic circumstances in the context). مر او را is an old-form pronominal construction meaning “to him”/“for him” (مر = to me/for me in the lemma list but context gives ‘to him’ with او را); یکی شادکام follows as an apposition: یکی “one/a certain” + شادکام “fortunate/fortunate in fate.”
segments.s519-g4: This is a string of epithets describing that son: خردمند “wise” + و (and) + جنگی “warlike/warrior” + و + ساسان “Sasan” with the phrase به نام ‘by name’ (به + نام) indicating the following is the name. The order is adjective(s) then the proper name with the standard preposition به before نام to mark naming.
segments.s519-g5: پدر را is the object with the definite marker را (‘his father’ as the thing seen). بران گونه is a fixed phrase meaning ‘in that manner’ or ‘in that way’ (بران = ‘thereon/upon that’, گونه = ‘manner’). چون here introduces the temporal/causal sense ‘when/as’ before the verb. کشته دید is simple past: کشته (killed) + دید (saw) = ‘saw (him) killed’ or ‘saw that he had been killed.’
segments.s519-g6: سر بخت ایرانیان is a genitival phrase: سر here carries the sense ‘head/chief’ (the leading part) and بخت ‘fortune/fate,’ together ‘the chief fortune (of) the Iranians’ (i.e., the Iranians’ luck/fate). گشته دید repeats the sensing verb with a perfect/turning sense: گشته (perfect participle ‘become/turned’) + دید (saw) — ‘he saw that (their) fortune had turned’ (i.e., their luck had changed).
segments.s519-g7: ازان لشکر روم is a prepositional phrase ‘from that army of Rome’ (ازان = from that/of those). لشکر روم is ‘the Roman host/army.’ بگریخت is the past tense verb ‘fled’ (ب- prefix + گریخت ‘fled’). اوی is an archaic/pronominal enclitic meaning ‘he’ (subject pronoun placed after the verb in epic register).
segments.s519-g8: به دام بلا در نیاویخت is a compact clause: به دام بلا در = ‘into the trap/snare of calamity’ (به + دام = into the trap; بلا = calamity; در = in). نیاویخت is a negative/nuanced past form meaning ‘did not get caught / was not ensnared’ (the verbal root آویخت ‘to cling/be caught’ with negative particle/preterit form ni-), and اوی again is the pronoun ‘he.’ The clause therefore says he did not fall/be caught in that snare.
segments.s519-g57: چو is a temporal conjunction here meaning "when." بابک is the subject; شنید is simple past "heard." این سخن is the object phrase "these/this words/speech." گشت + شاد is a consecutive predicate: گشت (became) + شاد (happy) — a common classical Persian pattern meaning "he became happy" after the hearing.
segments.s519-g58: براندازه‌شان is an adverbial phrase (براندازه + the possessive -شان) meaning "in proportion to them/their rank." یک به یک "one by one" modifies the giving action. هدیه داد is the past verb phrase (داد = "gave") with هدیه (gift) as object; the overall order places the manner/adverbials before the verb phrase, typical of Persian.
segments.s519-g59: بفرمود is an honorific past verb meaning "he ordered/commanded/said" (polite narrative past). تا introduces a subordinate clause expressing the content/purpose of the order ("that ..."). سرشبان از رمه specifies the person required: "the head-groom (سرشبان) from the flock (رمه)"; از marks origin/association here ("of/from the flock").
segments.s519-g60: بر + بابک indicates direction/toward Babak (i.e., "to Babak"). آید is the present/optative form "come" used in subordinate clauses after تا to express what should happen ("be brought/come"). به‌روز دمه is a temporal phrase "at the day's moment" i.e. "at once/at that very moment," functioning as an adverbial of time.
segments.s519-g61: بیامد is past "came." The verb-first order (بیامد شبان ...) is common in classical Persian narrative. پیش او = "before/in the presence of him" (i.e., "before him"). با + گلیم marks the accompaniment or instrument: "with a kilim."
segments.s519-g62: پر از = "full of" (پر followed by از + noun). برف is the noun filling it ("snow"). پشمینه (literally "woolen / woolen cloak") names another item the shepherd has. دل بدو نیم is an elliptical, poetic noun phrase: دل ("heart") + بدو ("to him") + نیم ("half") — i.e. "half his heart to him," where the verb "gave/offered" is understood from context (elliptical object construction common in poetry).
segments.s519-g63: بپردخت is past "he paid/gave" (here "rewarded/granted"). The following noun phrase is arranged in poetic order: بابک is the subject; ز بیگانه جای functions as the beneficiary/recipient phrase — poetically expressed as "a place for the stranger" (i.e., Babak gave the stranger a place/position).
segments.s519-g64: بدر here is used as a proper name (a person who was rewarded). شد is the past of "become." پرستنده and رهنمای are predicate nouns joined by و: "he became (or was) a devotee/worshipper and a guide." The clause means that Badr (the man) took on the roles of devoted follower and guide to Babak.
segments.s519-g65: ز ساسان بپرسید و بنواختش is a compact past narrative clause: ز ساسان (ز = از, poetic 'from') marks the source of the question, بپرسید is a past-tense verb 'he asked' with the subject omitted (third person). The conjunction و links to بنواختش, a past-tense verb with object suffix -ش 'him' meaning 'he made him play' / 'he caused him to sound (an instrument)'; both verbs share the same omitted subject.
segments.s519-g66: بر خویش نزدیک بنشاختش is a locative/result clause: بر خویش نزدیک = 'near himself' (خویش = 'his own/self' formed from خوی + possessive), and بنشاختش is a past verb with the accusative suffix -ش 'him', meaning 'he seated/placed him'. Word order is preposition/locative → verb; the action refers back to the same subject as the previous clause.
segments.s519-g67: بپرسیدش از گوهر و از نژاد: the verb بپرسیدش ('asked him') carries the object in the enclitic -ش. The two prepositional phrases از گوهر and از نژاد both use از 'concerning/about' to mark topics of interrogation ('about the gem' and 'about the lineage'), and و simply links the two complements.
segments.s519-g68: شبان زو بترسید و پاسخ نداد: شبان 'the shepherd' is the subject; ز (here fused as ز + او = 'from him') marks what/who causes the fear, and بترسید is a past-tense verb 'he feared' (or 'he was afraid of'). The clause is then linked with و to the coordinated clause پاسخ نداد 'he did not give an answer' (پاسخ = 'reply', نداد = 'did not give').
segments.s519-g69: ازان پس بدو گفت کای شهریار: ازان پس = 'after that/thereupon'; بدو گفت is a simple past clause 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him'). کای شهریار is a vocative phrase 'O king' (کای = vocative 'O'). Note: گفت is used as the reporting verb introducing direct speech.
segments.s519-g70: شبان را به جان‌گر دهی زینهار is the content of the admonition: شبان را (را marks the definite object 'the shepherd'), به جان‌گر دهی is a clause with دهی (second-person present/subjunctive/imperative 'you give') and به + name/title جان‌گر 'to Jan‑gar' (or 'into the care of the life‑giver'); زینهار is an interjection 'beware' or 'take heed' that follows the instruction. The verb دهی addresses the king directly (imperative/conditional sense).
segments.s519-g71: بگوید ز گوهر همه هرچ هست: بگوید is a hortative/indirect imperative form meaning 'tell' / 'let him say' in reported speech (here functioning as an instruction). ز گوهر = 'about the jewels', همه هرچ هست = 'all, whatever there is'; the clause orders a full disclosure about gems and whatever else exists.
segments.s519-g72: چو دستم بگیری به پیمان به دست is a temporal/conditional clause introduced by چو 'when/if'. دستم بگیری = 'you take my hand' (دستم 'my hand' with object), به پیمان 'as a pledge' (پیمان = 'covenant/pledge'), and the final به دست 'by/with hand' reiterates the physical act (instrumental/poetic reinforcement). The verbs are in the subjunctive/second-person form bگیری 'you take'.
segments.s519-g25: شبی is an adverbial temporal phrase 'one night'. خفته is a participial/adjectival form 'asleep', modifying the following noun; بد here is an adverbial/qualifying word ('badly' / 'ill' / 'deeply') qualifying خفته. بابک is the proper-name subject. The final words رود یاب are uncertain in this manuscript line: رود could be an archaic verbal form ('he went') or a corrupted token, and یاب looks like a root meaning 'find/obtain' or an imperative — the sequence is irregular and likely corrupt/elliptical; therefore I note the reading is problematic and its exact syntactic role is unclear (possibly a verb cluster or damaged phrase).
segments.s519-g26: چنان is a demonstrative/adverb 'so/thus'. دید is the past tense 'he saw' (simple perfect). روشن qualifies روانش: روشن روانش = 'his bright/clear soul/spirit' where -ش is the 3rd-person possessive clitic. به خاب is an archaic prepositional phrase 'in (his) sleep' (به + خاب, classical spelling of خواب). The overall order places the manner/adverb + verb before the phrase specifying what was seen.
segments.s519-g27: که introduces a subordinate/relative clause 'that/when'. ساسان is the subject proper name. به + پیل here marks the target of mounting ('onto the elephant'); classical Persian often uses به with a noun to indicate direction/attachment. ژیان is an epithet attached to پیل (literary 'living/huge' or 'vital'), and برنشست is the past compound verb 'he sat upon / mounted' (بر + نشست).
segments.s519-g28: یکی is an indefinite determiner 'a certain/one'. تیغ هندی is the noun phrase 'an Indian sword' (هندی qualifying تیغ). گرفته is a past participle 'having taken / seized' that modifies an implicit actor (here Sasan). به دست is a prepositional phrase 'in (his) hand' following the participle and indicating manner/position — the sequence means 'having taken an Indian sword in his hand.'
segments.s519-g29: هرانکس is the indefinite relative 'whoever/anyone who'; که is the relative particle 'who/that'; آمد is the simple past 'came'; بر او is a prepositional phrase 'to/against him' (here 'before him'); فراز is an adverb/noun meaning 'on high / up above' describing the place of approach. Together this is a standard relative clause 'whoever came before him on high.'
segments.s519-g30: آفرین کرد is a light-verb construction: آفرین (an acclamation 'praise/bravo') functions as the object/lexical nucleus and کرد is the verb 'he made/did' → 'he praised/acclaimed'. The initial برو is problematic in isolation (surface form can mean 'go' or be an interjection) and may be an editorial corruption or an exclamatory particle; the exact function here is unclear. و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'; بردش is a contracted form 'برد + ش' meaning 'took him' (برد + third-person clitic), and نماز is the noun 'prayer' — the phrase likely indicates that he acclaimed them and then 'took them with prayer' (i.e., sent them off or received them with prayers). Because برو is ambiguous in this line, the clause reading is somewhat uncertain but the central grammatical pattern is: [he] praised and [he] took/carried him with prayer.
segments.s519-g31: زمین is the direct object 'the earth/land' and is marked by the definite-object marker را. به خوبی is an instrumental/qualifying phrase 'with goodness' (به + noun functioning adverbially). بیاراستی is a classical past form 'you adorned' (second-person past) — in Ferdowsi's diction verbs of this pattern express completed action directed by the subject 'you' (the addressee/narratee).
segments.s519-g32: دل تیره is a noun phrase 'the heart dark/bleak' (تیره qualifying دل). از غم is a prepositional phrase 'from/of sorrow' specifying the cause of the darkness. بپیراستی is a verb form parallel to بیاراستی (second-person past) meaning 'you adorned / you made (it) fair' — here used of the heart, so the clause reads 'you adorned the heart that was dark from sorrow.' The verb form b- prefix is classical/poetic.
segments.s519-g17: بدو = an adverbial phrase 'to him' (به + او contracted), placed before the verb for 'he said to him'. گفت here is the verb 'said' (classical usage), with the indirect-object phrase preceding it. مزدورت is مزدور + the enclitic second‑person suffix -ت ('your hireling'); آید is the present/future form 'comes/will come' (classical present used to report an expected arrival), and به کار = 'to/for the work' (به + کار). The sequence literally: 'to him said: your-hireling comes to the work.'
segments.s519-g18: که begins a subordinate/relative clause ('that/which' or 'so that') linked to the previous clause; ایدر is an archaic/poetic form of این‌جا 'here'. گذارد (گذاردن) is third‑person singular 'puts/places' (classical present/imperative flavor depending on context) meaning 'place/assign'. به here has the sense 'into/toward', so به بد روزگار = 'into bad times' or 'into ill-fortune.'
segments.s519-g19: بپذرفت is an archaic past/simple perfect 'he accepted' (ب + پذیرفت). بدبخت را is the definite/accusative object (را marks the definite object): 'the unfortunate (man)'. سرشبان here identifies the agent/role (the head‑shepherd) — word order places the verb before the object and the agent/predicate after, a common classical ordering: 'the head‑shepherd accepted the unfortunate.'
segments.s519-g20: همی داشت is a classical progressive/periphrastic construction: همی + داشت gives 'he kept/continued to have' or 'he maintained (him)'. با expresses accompaniment 'with' (i.e. subjecting him to). رنج روز و شبان = 'the hardship/suffering of day and night' (روز = day; شبان here is poetic/pluralish for 'nights' or 'night‑work'); the whole phrase says he kept him with the toil of day and night.
segments.s519-g21: چو شد = 'when he became' (چو = when; شد = became). کارگر مرد is a compound: کارگر here = 'effective/capable' (adjective sense) and مرد = 'man' — together 'a capable man'. و آمد پسند = 'and he came into favor' or 'and was liked' (آمد + پسند (approval)), i.e. 'won approval.'
segments.s519-g22: شبان here = 'the shepherd' (the same person just mentioned). سرشبان گشت: گشت (گشتن) = 'became/turned into', so 'became head‑shepherd'. بر گوسفند = 'over the sheep' (بر + noun gives the sense 'over/above' the flock). The clause thus says the shepherd rose to be head shepherd of the sheep.
segments.s519-g23: دران روزگاری = archaic 'in those days' (دران = در + آن, روزگاری = 'times/days'). همی بود = 'there was' or 'there used to be' (همی adds a habitual/continuous nuance; بود is past 'was'). مرد = 'a man/person'. The whole segment sets the temporal frame: 'In those days there was a man...'.
segments.s519-g24: پر از = 'full of' (پر + از). غم دل = 'sorrow of the heart' (غم = sorrow; دل = heart). و تن پر از رنج و درد = 'and his body full of suffering and pain' (تن = body). The parallel structure repeats پر از ... to describe inner sorrow and bodily suffering.
segments.s519-g9: بدین phrase here reads as a short clause 'به هندوستان در به زاری بمرد' meaning 'he died in India in lamentation'. 'به هندوستان' uses the preposition به + place to indicate location/direction ('to/into/at India'). در after it doubles the locative emphasis ('in/within'); 'به زاری' is an adverbial phrase built with به + زاری meaning 'with/ in lamentation' (an adverbial use of به), and بمرد is an archaic past-form verb meaning 'he died' (classical/poetic morphology). Word order is subject omitted (understood from context) + locative/adverbials + verb (typical older Persian order).
segments.s519-g10: ز ساسان یکی کودکی ماند خرد = 'from Sasan one child remained small'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (indicating origin/lineage), یکی is the numeral/indefinite 'one', کودکی is a noun 'child' (object of ماند), ماند is the verb 'remained/was left' (past), and خرد follows as a postpositive adjective meaning 'small'/'young'. Note the older/poetic order: prepositional phrase + subject + verb + adjectival supplement.
segments.s519-g11: بدین هم‌نشان تا چهارم پسر = 'thus/with this corresponding (one), up to the fourth son'. بدین is a fused/formal adverb meaning 'with this' or 'thus' (ب‑ prefix + دین in older usage), هم‌نشان is an adjective/adverb meaning 'corresponding' or 'matching', and تا introduces the limit/extent 'up to/until' here governing 'چهارم پسر' ('the fourth son'). The phrase sets the frame for who is being named.
segments.s519-g12: همی نام ساسانش کردی پدر = 'the father named him Sasan'. همی is an adverbial particle used in classical Persian to emphasize or mark the action (here roughly 'thus/then'), نام is the noun 'name', ساسانش is 'Sasan' with the possessive/clitic -ش 'to him' (i.e. 'named him Sasan'), کردی is the past verb 'did/made/named' (classical past form), and پدر at the end is the subject 'the father' (older Persian often places the subject after the verb).
segments.s519-g13: شبانان بدندی و‌گر ساربان = 'the shepherds bared their teeth, and if (they were) camel-drivers'. شبانان is the plural subject 'shepherds'. بدندی is an older/plural past form meaning 'they bared (their) teeth' or 'they snarled/glared' (a metaphorical hostile gesture). و‌گر is a compound conjunction meaning 'and if' / 'but if', introducing the parallel case ساربان 'cameleer/caravan-leader'.
segments.s519-g14: همه‌ساله با رنج و کار گران = 'year after year with hardship and heavy work'. همه‌ساله is an adverb 'every year/annually', با + noun phrase gives the instrument/condition 'with hardship and work', and گران is a postpositive adjective 'heavy/arduous' qualifying کار. The whole phrase modifies the implied activity (they labored/endured).
segments.s519-g15: چو کهتر پسر سوی بابک رسید = 'when the younger son reached Babak'. چو is the temporal conjunction 'when', کهتر (literally 'smaller') functions adjectivally as 'younger' modifying پسر 'son', سوی + proper name indicates direction 'to/toward Babak', and رسید is the past verb 'arrived/reached'. The clause follows classical word order: temporal conjunction + noun phrase + verb.
segments.s519-g16: به دشت اندرون سر شبان را بدید = 'he saw the shepherds' chiefs in the inner plain'. به دشت اندرون is a locative phrase 'to/at the inner/plain within the plain' (به marking direction/location, اندرون 'inner/within'), سر شبان را is the definite object with را where سر means 'chief/leader' (here 'the leaders of the shepherds'), and بدید is the past verb 'saw' (classical form). Note the object marker را and the postpositive adjective/order typical of older Persian.
segments.s519-g89: بدو is the fused preposition + pronoun (به + او) meaning 'to him/for him' and appears before the verb; گفت is the past tense 'said' (verb) with the subject بابک following it (classical/poetic word order allows verb before subject). به گرمابه شو is an imperative command: به marks direction 'to', گرمابه 'the public bath', and شو is the imperative of شدن (here used idiomatically as 'go/become there' → 'go/enter').
segments.s519-g90: همی باش combines the particle همی (continuous/emphatic marker) with باش (imperative 'be/stay') to mean 'remain/stay (there)'; تا introduces a temporal subordinate clause 'until'. خلعت is 'robe of honour' (direct object of the following verb), آرند is the 3rd-person plural present/subjunctive form of آوردن 'to bring' (used here as 'they bring'), and نو is the adjective 'new' modifying خلعت. Word order places the object (خلعت) before the verb (آرند) in this poetic line.
segments.s519-g91: یکی here is an indefinite subject 'a certain one / someone'. کاخ پرمایه is a noun+modifier phrase 'a substantial / richly endowed palace' (poetic ordering). او را (with را) marks the beneficiary/indirect object 'for him'. بساخت is the past tense 'built' (transitive verb placed at the end of the clause).
segments.s519-g92: ازان introduces source/selection 'from those / of those'; سرشبانان is the plural 'head-grooms / equerries'. سرش is سر + enclitic ش (possessive) here functioning as 'their/head' in the sense of 'a head/chief for them' (i.e., he set someone up as chief of those equerries). برافراخت is the past 'raised/erected' used figuratively 'elevated (to the headship)'. Poetic word order allows the nominal (سرش) before the verb.
segments.s519-g93: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. او را with را is the direct/foregrounded object 'him'. بران (بر + آن contracted) marks location 'in/on that (place)'. کاخ is 'palace' and بر جای کردن is an idiomatic verbal construction 'to put/set in place / to install', with کرد the past auxiliary verb 'did/made'. The clause means 'when he installed him in that palace'.
segments.s519-g94: غلام و پرستنده are coordinated nouns 'slaves and devotees/attendants' (objects/people assigned). بر پای کردن is an idiom meaning 'to set/position (someone) at the feet (of another)' — i.e., to station them as attendants at his feet — and کرد is the past-tense 'made/placed'. The phrase therefore says he stationed slaves and attendants to wait at his feet.
segments.s519-g95: به هر آلتی is a prepositional phrase 'in/to every instrument/way' used idiomatically 'in every respect / by every means'. سرفرازیش is سرفراز ('exalted, honored') with the 3rd-person possessive clitic ش = 'his exaltation/glory', and داد is the past 'gave' — together 'he gave him honor/exaltation in every way.'
segments.s519-g96: هم 'also' / 'too' adds the second clause. از introduces the source/thing he is freed from ('from'), خواسته is 'desire/wish', بی‌نیازیش is the adjective بی‌نیاز 'free from need' with the clitic ش 'his' → 'his freedom from need', and داد again 'gave'. The whole clause means he also made him free from need — i.e. supplied his wants or gave him everything he sought.

sentences.s519-s49: He then gave him his chosen daughter and his crown.
sentences.s519-s41: Remember the scion of the world-ruler King Ardashir whom you call Bahman.
sentences.s519-s42: Esfandiyar, the honored son and hero, sprung from Gushtasp — a hero and a remembrance in the world.
sentences.s519-s43: When Babak heard it, water poured down from that bright eye which he had seen in a dream.
sentences.s519-s44: Then he brought a Pahlavi robe at once, together with the royal implements.
sentences.s519-s25: The guide became pensive at that speech, and (having) set his ear toward the responder.
sentences.s519-s26: At last he said, 'O exalted king, this must be understood in this interpretation.'
sentences.s519-s27: Someone who is seen in a dream in this way will be raised to kingship, his head reaching the sun.
sentences.s519-s28: But if, by chance, this dream passes away from him, he will have a son who will fall upon (or seize) the world.
sentences.s519-s37: That you will not do me harm in the world, neither openly nor secretly.
sentences.s519-s38: When Babak heard, he opened his tongue; he remembered the gift of goodness from God.
sentences.s519-s39: That I will not cause you any harm, I will keep you joyful‑hearted and honoured.
sentences.s519-s40: Thereafter the young man said to Babak thus: “For I am a son/descendant of Sasan, O champion.”
sentences.s519-s21: Babak's head woke from sleep; his spirit and heart were filled with care.
sentences.s519-s22: Whoever is wise in sleep, to every kind of knowledge they would be able.
sentences.s519-s23: An assembly gathered on Babak’s iwan: the great, wise men and those who gave counsel.
sentences.s519-s24: When Babak opened his speech and revealed what was hidden, he told them the whole dream at once.
sentences.s519-s17: On another night, when Babak fell asleep, his mind was always paired with thought.
sentences.s519-s18: Thus in his sleep he saw a fire‑worshipper who carried three blazing fires in his hand.
sentences.s519-s19: Like the Azar of Goshasp, and like Khraad and Mehr, they were radiant as the revolving heavens.
sentences.s519-s20: He set them all, radiant, before Sasan; to each fire he gave (offered) burning agarwood.
sentences.s519-s1: When Dārā was slain in the fighting within, the day turned back over all the soot.
sentences.s519-s2: To him remained a son — one fortunate, wise and warlike — named Sasan.
sentences.s519-s3: When he saw his father slain in that manner, he perceived that the Iranians' fortune had turned.
sentences.s519-s4: From that Roman host he fled; he did not fall into the snare of calamity.
sentences.s519-s29: When Babak heard these words he grew glad, and he gave gifts to each one, one by one, according to their rank.
sentences.s519-s30: He commanded that the chief shepherd of the flock be brought to Babak at once (at that moment).
sentences.s519-s31: The shepherd came before him with a kilim; it was full of snow, (and) a woolen cloak and half his heart he brought to him.
sentences.s519-s32: Babak rewarded the stranger with a place; Badr became his devotee and guide.
sentences.s519-s33: He asked Sasan and made him play, then seated him close beside himself.
sentences.s519-s34: He asked him about the jewel and about his lineage; the shepherd feared him and gave no answer.
sentences.s519-s35: After that he said to him, 'O king, beware if you give the shepherd to Jan‑gar.'
sentences.s519-s36: Tell him, concerning every jewel and whatever there is, when you take my hand as a pledge with your hand.
sentences.s519-s13: One night, while Babak lay asleep, he saw in his sleep a spirit so bright.
sentences.s519-s14: When Sasan mounted a mighty elephant, he took an Indian sword in his hand.
sentences.s519-s15: Whoever came before him up on high, he praised (acclaimed) and, with prayer, carried him off.
sentences.s519-s16: You adorned the earth with goodness; you adorned the heart, darkened by sorrow.
sentences.s519-s9: He said to him, "Send your hireling to do the work here; put him into ill fortune (into these hard times)."
sentences.s519-s10: The head‑shepherd accepted the unfortunate man; he kept him to endure the hardships of day and night.
sentences.s519-s11: When he became a capable man and won approval, the shepherd became head‑shepherd over the sheep.
sentences.s519-s12: In those days there was a man whose heart was full of sorrow and whose body was full of suffering and pain.
sentences.s519-s5: He died in India amid lamentation; from Sasan one small child remained.
sentences.s519-s6: Thus, in the same fashion, the father named the fourth son Sasan.
sentences.s519-s7: The shepherds bared their teeth; and the camel-drivers, year after year, endured heavy toil and hardship.
sentences.s519-s8: When the younger son reached Babak, he came to the inner plain and saw the shepherds' leaders.
sentences.s519-s45: Babak said to him, "Go to the bath and remain there until they bring a new robe."
sentences.s519-s46: A certain person built for him a richly endowed palace, and from among those equerries he raised a head (a chief).
sentences.s519-s47: When he placed him in that palace, he set slaves and devoted attendants to stand at his feet.
sentences.s519-s48: He granted him honor in every respect, and also relieved him of all need (gave him whatever he desired).
